topic: Religion, Philosophy & Ethics: Dialogue Between Religious & Non-Religious Beliefs
subject: religious-studies

items.q: What is the focus of virtue ethics?
items.correct: On character and habits — becoming the kind of person who acts well, aiming at eudaimonia (flourishing) through practising virtues that lie between extremes.
items.misconception: Virtue ethics tells you which specific actions are right and wrong.
items.random_wrong: Virtue ethics is about following the Ten Commandments.
items.why: Virtue ethics asks 'what kind of person should I be?' rather than 'what should I do?'. Students try to extract action-rules from it, missing its character-based agent-focused nature.

items.correct: Five distinct arguments for God's existence: from motion, causation, contingency, gradation of being, and final causes/teleology — collectively forming Aquinas's natural theology, not a single argument.
items.misconception: Aquinas's Five Ways are five steps of one cosmological argument.
items.random_wrong: Aquinas's Five Ways are five Christian sacraments.
items.why: Each Way is a self-standing argument with different structure. Students treat them as a single chain, missing that Aquinas offered multiple independent routes — the failure of one doesn't undermine the others.

items.correct: It is more accurately described as henotheistic or monistic — many gods (devas) are considered aspects or manifestations of one ultimate reality (Brahman).
items.misconception: Hinduism is straightforwardly polytheistic with millions of separate gods.
items.random_wrong: Hinduism worships no gods, only ancestors.
items.why: The 'many gods' surface obscures the underlying unity in much Hindu theology. Many traditions hold all deities to be expressions of one Brahman — students miss the monistic underpinning.
It's Rahner. “Hick” is the classic near-miss here; knowing why it's wrong is worth as much as knowing the right answer.

items.q: Which Vatican II document addressed other religions?
items.why: It's Nostra Aetate. “Dei Verbum” is the classic near-miss here; knowing why it's wrong is worth as much as knowing the right answer.

items.q: In what year was Nostra Aetate promulgated?
items.why: The real figure is 1965 AD. The wrong options cluster deliberately close — anchor the exact number to one concrete fact so it can't drift.
